The present report contains the budget performance and the budget for the United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in the Central African Republic (MINUSCA) for the periods from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 and from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027, respectively.

During the 2024/25 period, MINUSCA advanced the implementation of its political strategy in close coordination with the Government of the Central African Republic. The Mission supported preparations for the electoral process and facilitated the implementation of an agreement signed on 19 April 2025 with two major armed groups, enabling progress in disarmament and demobilization and contributing to the revitalization of the Political Agreement for Peace and Reconciliation in the Central African Republic. In parallel, joint operations with national defence and security forces helped to reduce the presence of armed groups, reinforce border security and enhance the protection of civilians. Further progress was recorded in the development of early warning mechanisms and in the monitoring and reporting of human rights violations and abuses.

State authority and governance were reinforced through a decentralization approach, including the deployment of local officials, infrastructure rehabilitation, and capacity-building in the police, justice and penitentiary sectors, supported by partnerships with national and regional actors. Operational capacity was strengthened through enhanced airlift and mobility, an expanded field presence and increased local procurement. Environmental sustainability advanced through the installation of solar systems, generator upgrades and waste management initiatives. Despite persistent financial and political constraints, the Mission maintained progress towards improved security, governance and conditions conducive to credible and inclusive elections.

Looking ahead to the 2026/27 period, MINUSCA will focus on consolidating the considerable, though still fragile, gains and safeguarding these achievements to ensure sustainability, prevent a relapse into conflict and leave a positive legacy. The Mission will continue to implement its political strategy and integrated mission plan, with sustained efforts to advance the Political Agreement through political dialogue; reconciliation; disarmament, demobilization and reintegration; community violence reduction; and security sector reform, while continuing to protect civilians.

MINUSCA operations will remain shaped by regional dynamics, armed group activity, post-election tensions and economic challenges, requiring integrated political, security and programmatic responses. The Mission will maintain operational readiness and further strengthen State authority and local governance through decentralization, enhanced rule of law and human rights protection, while reinforcing coordination with national and international partners. MINUSCA will also continue to identify and implement efficiency measures, organizational optimization and functional streamlining, including through reconfiguration and consolidation.

MINUSCA incurred $1,146.3 million in expenditure for the budget performance period, representing a resource utilization rate of 97.8 per cent, compared with $1,143.0 million in expenditure and a resource utilization rate of 99.8 per cent in the 2023/24 period.

The unencumbered balance of $25.4 million reflects the net impact of the following: (a) reduced requirements for military and police personnel, attributable primarily to the repatriation and delayed rotation of units, the non-deployment or partial non-functionality of major equipment and lower ration costs; (b) increased requirements for civilian personnel, attributable primarily to lower actual average vacancy rates for international and national staff, higher actual common staff costs, and the revised higher national staff salary scale; and (c) reduced requirements for operational costs, primarily under air operations, attributable to the delayed or partial deployment of helicopters, lower utilization of air ambulance services, the unavailability of one fixed-wing aircraft and lower volumes and prices of aviation fuel.

The budget proposed for the 2026/27 period, in the amount of $1,021.6 million, reflects a decrease of $152.5 million, or 13.0 per cent, compared with the apportionment of $1,174.1 million approved for the 2025/26 period.

The estimates for the 2026/27 period are aligned with the Mission’s strategic priorities and operational needs and reflect a balance of efficiency-driven reductions and operationally necessary increases, as follows: (a) under military and police personnel, reductions result primarily from the application of higher vacancy rates; and (b) under civilian personnel, decreases stem from the proposed abolishment of 151 posts and positions, including election-related temporary positions following the scheduled completion of the Mission’s electoral mandate by 30 June 2026, alongside higher vacancy rates across most staff categories. Through the streamlining of functions and the optimization of resource allocation, the Mission will refine its footprint, strengthen the prioritization of critical mandated activities and ensure the continued effectiveness of Mission operations.

text: I.	Mandate, results achieved and planned outcome

children.text: A.	Mandate

1.	The mandate of the United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in the Central African Republic (MINUSCA) was established by the Security Council in its resolution 2149 (2014). The mandate for the performance period was provided in its resolutions 2709 (2023) and 2759 (2024). The most recent extension of the mandate was authorized by the Council in its resolution 2800 (2025), by which the Council extended the mandate until 15 November 2026.

2.	In its resolution 2800 (2025), the Security Council reduced the authorized strength of the uniformed component of the Mission by deciding that MINUSCA shall comprise up to 14,046 military personnel, including 580 military observers and military staff officers, and 2,999 police personnel, including 589 individual police officers and 2,410 formed police unit personnel, as well as 108 corrections officers, recalled its intention to keep that number under continuous review, taking into account progress on the security situation and the objective of transition and eventual drawdown of MINUSCA when conditions were met, and further expressed its firm intention to review the number of personnel following the successful completion of the electoral process scheduled in 2025 and 2026.
3.	The Mission is mandated to help the Security Council achieve the overall objective of advancing peace and security in the Central African Republic.
B.	Budget implementation for 2024/25

4.	During the reporting period, MINUSCA advanced the implementation of its mission plan through strengthened coordination with the Government of the Central African Republic, fostering inclusive dialogue, supporting stabilization efforts and assisting with electoral preparations, despite the postponement of local elections. The Mission contributed to the revision of the voter list and supported the implementation of a landmark agreement signed on 19 April 2025 between the Government and two major armed groups, Union pour la paix en Centrafrique (UPC) and Retour, réclamation et réhabilitation (3R). This agreement enabled renewed engagement with the Political Agreement for Peace and Reconciliation in the Central African Republic and facilitated the disarmament and demobilization of combatants. In volatile areas, MINUSCA joint operations with national forces curtailed armed group influence, expanded protection measures and strengthened border security. These operations, complemented by explosive ordnance risk education, human rights monitoring and support for local peace and mediation mechanisms, helped to create conditions conducive to the extension of State authority and contributed to improved security.

5.	State authority and governance were reinforced through decentralization initiatives, the deployment of local officials, capacity-building in the justice sector and infrastructure rehabilitation. Justice institutions, including the Special Criminal Court, progressively expanded their operations nationwide, although progress in transitional justice remained constrained. The Mission advanced security sector reform and the protection of civilians through a whole-of-mission approach. Operational capacity was strengthened through enhanced mobility, information and communications technology upgrades, environmental measures and targeted support for defence, security and justice institutions. Partnerships with the United Nations country team and regional organizations underpinned efforts to extend State authority and sustain peace. Persistent violations, weak institutions, limited fiscal capacity and uneven political will to pursue reforms continued to constrain progress. While the N’Djamena Agreement offered new prospects for revitalizing the peace process, the approach of national elections amid political divisions underscored the need for intensified good offices and an adjusted Mission posture to foster conditions conducive to credible, inclusive and peaceful elections.

6.	The implementation of the Mission’s mandate was constrained by financial, operational, infrastructure-related and political challenges. Liquidity constraints required cost-containment measures, including the suspension of some travel, procurement delays and strict prioritization of mandated tasks. Critical fuel shortages necessitated mobility restrictions, which adversely affected operational capacity and activities related to the protection of civilians. The liquidity constraints limited the decentralization of operations and the expansion of field presence, while prolonged liquidity pressures negatively affected staff well-being, particularly in remote field offices. Weak national infrastructure, inadequate service delivery and restricted accessibility further hindered both Mission activities and public access to essential services. Fiscal limitations and competing national priorities slowed the pace of mandate implementation and required the Mission to adapt its approach while maintaining progress toward strategic objectives. In response, MINUSCA pursued more cost-efficient means of mandate delivery while encouraging greater national ownership by the Government.

7.	MINUSCA supported conditions conducive to the holding of local elections through good offices, as well as technical, logistical, operational and security assistance, with a focus on the revision of the voter list. Despite the postponement of local elections, the Mission provided nationwide planning, technical, training, transport and security support for voter registration, including in remote areas, and conducted civic education and outreach activities. These efforts contributed to the completion of the voter list revision in August 2025 and laid the groundwork for subsequent electoral processes.

8.	The Mission enhanced mobility, flexibility and operational posture through the deployment of targeted enabling capabilities. Uniformed components benefited from air assets, the concentration of forces, the rationalization of operating bases and the construction or rehabilitation of critical infrastructure. These measures enabled proactive operations, strengthened the protection of civilians and improved humanitarian access. MINUSCA enhanced situational awareness and early warning capacity through the implementation of the peacekeeping intelligence action plan and early warning systems. Progress across the six pillars of the mission plan included rolling out integrated peacekeeping intelligence coordination mechanisms to harmonize the analysis of threats, link assessments to strategic decision-making through monthly forecasts, and conduct peacekeeping intelligence training jointly with the Peacekeeping-Intelligence Academy. A pilot project introducing artificial intelligence to analytical capabilities was also initiated, along with enhanced exchanges with neighbouring peacekeeping missions.

9.	The Mission continued to implement infrastructure projects facilitating access, including the construction and rehabilitation of roads, bridges and ferries. These initiatives, coordinated with humanitarian actors and the United Nations country team, improved mobility, access to essential services and the safe return, integration or resettlement of internally displaced persons and refugees. It also strengthened early warning and response capacities, coordination between security and humanitarian actors, and State presence in previously isolated areas, thereby enhancing the protection of civilians and community resilience.

10.	MINUSCA supported the drafting and adoption of the national defence policy and strategy and the border management policy, and the development of the 2026–2030 military programming law, strengthening long-term defence planning and nationwide force deployment. The Mission maintained its support for internal oversight and military justice, contributing to accountability and efforts to combat impunity. Parallel support for national judicial and corrections institutions enabled an increase in the number of cases brought to a conclusion, while the Special Criminal Court pursued its mandate across multiple locations.

11.	The Mission assisted national authorities in improving weapons and ammunition management through infrastructure upgrades, training and technical assistance. These activities enhanced safe storage, built national capacity, including for women personnel, and reinforced compliance through joint quality-assurance visits. MINUSCA also supported the implementation of the national action plan on small arms and light weapons, including marking, registration and the establishment of regional offices of the national commission, contributing to decentralization objectives and extending State authority in the regions.

12.	MINUSCA prioritized mobility, performance optimization and accountability, with particular emphasis on enforcing the United Nations zero-tolerance policy on sexual exploitation and abuse. Key actions included timely training for personnel, targeted support to high-risk contingents, integrated risk assessments of base camps, expanded awareness campaigns, community engagement initiatives and joint military-police deterrent patrols. Co-location of police and civilian staff with military personnel promoted integrated field presence and helped mitigate risks. Victims received comprehensive support, including counselling, mental health services and access to vocational training through established programmes.

13.	During the reporting period, the Mission further consolidated its digital management environment by aligning planning, budgeting and performance platforms within a single framework. This enabled synchronization between the mission plan results framework and budget reporting. Analytical dashboards tracked financial allocations against projects by section and location, thereby enhancing transparency and the visibility of data to senior leadership. Leveraging Comprehensive Planning and Performance Assessment System tools, the Mission produced visual products, including infographics, to clearly illustrate progress, trends and challenges.

14.	MINUSCA implemented an integrated, multi-platform communications and outreach strategy to support mandate delivery and strengthen public understanding of priorities, including the protection of civilians, peace process support, extension of State authority and humanitarian facilitation. Coordinated with the Government and national and local media, these efforts enhanced visibility, reinforced impartiality, countered misinformation and built community trust, including through targeted radio campaigns in high-risk areas.

15.	The Mission progressively adjusted its field presence to align with new administrative boundaries, enabling co-location of uniformed and civilian personnel and strengthening regional coordination. This decentralized posture was complemented by the phased reconfiguration of force and police components, taking into account evolving security conditions, national force capacities and logistical constraints. Rationalization of the footprint preserved the Mission’s ability to protect civilians, maintain operational agility and support priority mandate tasks.

16.	MINUSCA strengthened the collection, analysis and sharing of information through improved coordination between Mission headquarters and field offices, enabling timely and actionable early warning. Information management and analysis were aligned with the Mission’s four-sector force structure, allowing for a stronger sector-level focus while maintaining Mission headquarters-level coordination and leadership support. Key functional areas continued to provide intelligence-informed support to operations and protection activities, improving situational awareness and enabling proactive responses to emerging threats.

17.	MINUSCA implemented infrastructure projects to improve living and working conditions and sustain operational readiness. Construction and extension works at Bangassou Sector headquarters reached approximately 14 per cent completion before they were suspended due to liquidity constraints. Essential maintenance at airfields in Obo, Kaga Bandoro and Bossangoa preserved air mobility. The Mission supported the redeployment and operational readiness of vetted internal security forces, including transportation and the construction and rehabilitation of facilities, enabling the deployment of over 300 personnel and the construction or rehabilitation of more than 60 police and gendarmerie offices. Support was provided to the Central African Armed Forces where feasible, with funds prioritized to achieve immediate operational impact.

18.	Local procurement increased significantly to $16.5 million, engaging the services of over 100 vendors, including 20 under long-term agreements. Air operations were enhanced through the deployment of Mi-8 and UH-60 helicopters and a C-130 aircraft, which delivered 9,462 tons of cargo across 12 sites, improving operational reach and responsiveness. Multi-vendor inland transport contracts cleared cargo backlogs and improved delivery timelines.

19.	Despite challenges from a six-month rainy season, MINUSCA enhanced mobility through infrastructure upgrades, bridge works and the maintenance of key supply routes and ferries. Decentralization was supported through the construction of facilities in Bangassou, Ndélé, Birao and Obo, and the completion of camps in Sam Ouandja and Ippy. Environmental sustainability advanced through the installation of seven solar photovoltaic systems (993 kilowatt-peak (kWp)), projected to save approximately 486,000 litres of fuel annually, the replacement of eight generators with more efficient models, and waste-management initiatives, including completion of the Bimbo waste yard phase one, ongoing maintenance of the Kolongo landfill and subsequent phases of those projects.
C.	Planning assumptions and mission support initiatives for 2026/27

20.	The successful conduct of the combined presidential, legislative, regional and municipal elections on 28 December 2025 marked a significant milestone for the Central African Republic and provides an opportunity for MINUSCA to adjust its footprint and posture in line with its mandate under Security Council resolution 2800 (2025). During the 2026/27 period, the Mission will advance internal planning and analysis processes to guide adaptation to the post-election environment, aligning its configuration with strategic priorities while identifying organizational, functional and process efficiencies to support sustainable mandate delivery. MINUSCA will focus on consolidating the considerable, though still fragile, gains and safeguarding these achievements to ensure sustainability, prevent a relapse into conflict and leave a positive legacy.

21.	The Mission will continue to implement its political strategy and integrated mission plan for 2023–2028, with a sustained focus on finalizing the implementation of the peace agreement, strengthening conflict-management mechanisms and supporting the extension of State presence and authority. Building on progress in strengthening national and local political processes, coordination frameworks and decentralized engagement, MINUSCA will prioritize coherence, field-level implementation and national ownership to support stabilization and the extension of State authority, gradually shifting from facilitation to the consolidation of political outcomes.

22.	In the light of the post-election context, and in preparation for implementation of the MINUSCA mandate during the 2026/27 period, the Mission is initiating a phase of consolidation, reconfiguration and internal strategic review, in line with paragraph 48 of Security Council resolution 2800 (2025). Following the successful conduct of national elections, and consistent with the Mission’s plans to expeditiously sunset the electoral division, the Mission has completed the withdrawal of all elections-related resources. The Mission has also begun assessing the evolving political and security environment to identify opportunities to adjust its footprint, refine its posture and seek efficiencies in the delivery of mandated activities. These efforts, however, are being undertaken against the backdrop of persistent liquidity constraints, which are delaying the initiation of planned activities and potentially constraining the Mission’s flexibility in implementing reconfiguration measures. As a result, consolidation and restructuring efforts will require strengthened prioritization and tighter controls to ensure that scarce resources are directed to the most critical mandate priorities, while positioning the Mission to sustain impact and effectiveness in the post-election environment.

23.	The Mission will continue to build on recent political and peace process gains, including renewed engagement by armed groups in the Political Agreement and strengthened regional and international cooperation. Efforts will focus on supporting inclusive dialogue, safeguarding civic and political space and upholding and promoting respect for human rights. MINUSCA will strengthen its integrated, multi-platform communications and outreach strategy to support mandate delivery, with a focus on coordinated messaging with the Government and national and local media, targeted radio campaigns in high-risk areas, and cost-effective dissemination to enhance public understanding, counter misinformation and reinforce community trust in mandate priorities.

24.	MINUSCA will continue to prioritize implementation of the Political Agreement, maintaining engagement with Union pour la paix en Centrafrique (UPC), Retour, réclamation et réhabilitation (3R) and other armed actors. The Mission will facilitate dialogue, monitor compliance and implement reconciliation initiatives to consolidate peace gains.

25.	Disarmament, demobilization and reintegration and community violence reduction programmes will be sustained and adapted to address remaining caseloads of former combatants and at-risk youth. Despite progress, incomplete reintegration and persistent socioeconomic vulnerabilities pose risks of renewed recruitment and localized violence, particularly in the post-election context. MINUSCA will work closely with newly elected authorities to ensure alignment with national priorities, including decentralization, strengthening local governance, and promoting human rights and the rule of law. The Mission will continue to empower local communities to take ownership of the peace process, enhance information-sharing and increase public awareness.

26.	Mission operations will be shaped by regional security dynamics, fluctuating armed group activity, post-election tensions and persistent logistical, economic and fiscal constraints, alongside socioeconomic vulnerabilities among at-risk populations, particularly young people. These conditions necessitate an integrated political, security and programmatic approach to preserve and consolidate stabilization gains and support reconciliation efforts. Maintaining a footprint that ensures operational readiness, sustained field presence and rapid response capacity is essential, while deployments will remain flexible and adaptive to evolving risks and constraints.

27.	Force and police components will continue implementing operations under the mission plan while optimizing the post-election footprint, accounting for evolving risks from armed group activity, residual post-election tensions, explosive ordnance threats and spoiler actions. Key uniformed capabilities, including quick reaction forces, aviation, engineering, intelligence, and agile light infantry remain essential to address operational gaps created by base closures, sustain deterrence, ensure mobility and maintain the protection of civilians and United Nations and humanitarian personnel. Quick reaction force units will establish short-term temporary operating bases and deploy within 24 to 48 hours to remote locations requiring air insertion. Aviation remains the primary mode of transportation to reach remote locations in all weather conditions. Combined aviation and quick reaction force capabilities have mitigated logistical gaps, enhanced casualty evacuation capacity and reinforced the Mission’s presence in higher-threat areas. MINUSCA will continue to support the Armed Forces of the Central African Republic and internal security forces through joint operations, mentoring, training and weapons and ammunition management, contributing to the progressive extension of State security presence and rule of law institutions, in compliance with the United Nations human rights due diligence policy. Complementary disarmament, demobilization and reintegration, community violence reduction and small arms control initiatives will continue to address the root causes of insecurity and support community stabilization.

28.	The continued presence of explosive ordnance threats remains a significant risk to civilian safety and freedom of movement, requiring sustained mitigation and prevention efforts. The Mission will support national forces and assist in operationalizing the national mine action authority to strengthen institutional frameworks and operational capacity for explosive ordnance management, complemented by ongoing risk education for affected communities. Systematic monitoring and reporting of human rights violations will remain central to the Mission’s protection-of-civilians approach, informing operational decision-making and guiding engagement and capacity-building with national authorities.

29.	The Mission will continue to prioritize high-risk areas and border regions through targeted deployments, security patrols, and operational support to the Armed Forces of the Central African Republic and internal security forces to safeguard civilians and personnel. Continuous threat assessments and reconnaissance tools will guide evidence-based operational adjustments and security planning. Cross-cutting operational assumptions, including logistical and security constraints such as fuel shortages, mobility limitations and liquidity pressures, will influence deployment flexibility. Conduct- and discipline-related risks, including sexual exploitation and abuse, misconduct and reputational challenges, will remain actively managed to preserve operational effectiveness and maintain trust with local communities and partners.

30.	MINUSCA will continue to work closely with the United Nations country team and international financial institutions to support decentralization and local governance initiatives through sustained guidance and capacity-building for local authorities, particularly in the context of electoral transitions. This support strengthens State presence and institutional capacity, mitigates risks of uneven decentralization and promotes good governance, democratic processes, human rights and effective service delivery, ensuring that post-election transitions translate into tangible stabilization and development outcomes.

31.	The consolidation of the rule of law and State authority will require integrated political, security and programmatic interventions, particularly in the context of reduced international support, economic pressures and persistent local vulnerabilities. Targeted support for the judiciary, including the Special Criminal Court and penitentiary institutions, will promote transparency, efficiency and accountability, while community engagement initiatives will strengthen trust between citizens and local authorities. Complementing these efforts, the Mission will maintain support for critical infrastructure, including administrative buildings, to facilitate personnel deployment, service delivery and the extension of State presence in underserved areas. Coordination with the United Nations country team, strategic partners and donors will remain essential to ensure coherent, mutually reinforcing interventions aligned with national priorities.
Mission support initiatives

32.	MINUSCA will continue to decentralize its operations and execute targeted construction projects aimed at improving the safety and living and working conditions of Mission personnel in the field. These efforts will be carefully managed in the context of ongoing liquidity constraints, with a focus on maximizing operational impact.

33.	The Mission will implement the priority infrastructure projects, including: (a) the installation of a hybrid solar photovoltaic system at Kaga Bandoro to increase the renewable energy share and reduce fuel utilization; (b) the continuation of bridge abutment construction; (c) the design and construction of a stormwater canal network; (d) the construction of concrete helipads in Zemio, Mboki and Bocaranga, which are essential for protection-of-civilian operations and casualty evacuation in volatile regions; and (e) the second phase of borehole drilling works.

34.	MINUSCA will continue to reduce its environmental footprint and align its operations with a long-term vision for a positive environmental legacy. The Mission will strengthen solid waste and wastewater management through ongoing monitoring and infrastructure development, including by acquiring solid waste equipment such as incinerators and glass crushers, installing wastewater treatment plants and upgrading existing facilities. In addition, Unite field remote infrastructure monitoring systems will continue to be deployed, enabling real-time data collection and proactive maintenance to support data-driven improvements in environmental performance.

35.	To enhance logistical efficiency and ensure reliable operational support, MINUSCA will maintain decentralized warehousing in high-demand regions, digitize supply chain processes with improved forecasting and risk monitoring and optimize the use of the C-130 Hercules aircraft for air cargo. Inventory and warehouse management will be strengthened through targeted training, emphasizing national capacity development. Enhanced monitoring systems will ensure that logistics operations remain agile, resilient and responsive to rapid decision-making.

36.	MINUSCA will continue to leverage technology and advance the digitalization of its operations through the systematic use of integrated platforms such as the Comprehensive Planning and Performance Assessment System, Unite Aware, Situational Awareness Geospatial Enterprise (SAGE) and Umoja to support planning, monitoring, reporting and analysis. These tools have streamlined and standardized internal processes, reduced reliance on paper and other consumables and improved the availability of information for management and decision-making. At the same time, the Mission has expanded virtual and hybrid meetings, minimizing travel and in-person engagements while maintaining effective coordination across components and with partners.

37.	In line with the Secretary-General’s UN80 Initiative, the proposed 2026/27 budget reflects a set of efficiency measures designed to enhance effectiveness through organizational optimization, functional streamlining and allocative efficiencies. These measures are informed by a review of operational requirements and workload distribution, and are implemented primarily through adjustments to force posture and a reduction in personnel, while maintaining the delivery of core mandated activities. Mitigating measures are being put in place to minimize adverse effects. Resources have been realigned to reflect evolving operational priorities, supported by optimized deployment, strengthened coordination, and adjustments to the sequencing and modalities of activities, enabling the Mission to sustain essential outcomes with a reduced footprint.

38.	Civilian staffing adjustments have been implemented through functional streamlining and organizational optimization, including the redistribution of responsibilities, the merging of functions and the refinement of internal workflows, with some functions absorbed by remaining posts. The proposed merger of the Joint Operations Centre and the Joint Mission Analysis Centre, alongside structural adjustments, will boost efficiency and performance. Needs for goods and services have been aligned with the reduced operational footprint while maintaining essential capabilities. While these measures require tighter prioritization and may reduce flexibility to respond to workload surges, the Mission has strengthened internal coordination, joint integrated planning, management oversight and prioritization mechanisms to mitigate risks and ensure effective mandate delivery.

39.	The Mission will continue to adjust its organizational structure to emphasize the centrality of security, the protection of civilians and the extension of State presence and authority across all components. Structural realignments, including the consolidation of coordination and analytical functions and the redeployment of resources under executive direction and management, aim to enhance field-level responsiveness, strengthen decentralized protection delivery and improve operational coherence under constrained resource conditions. These adjustments will support more efficient decision-making, clearer accountability and optimized use of existing capacities to deliver protection outcomes where they are most needed.

40.	In pursuit of allocative efficiencies, the Mission continues to realign resources with evolving operational and programmatic requirements, prioritizing substantive activities over administrative functions and evaluating deliverables against production costs to optimize the impact of planned outcomes. A structured resource allocation process, supported by high-level coordination meetings, ensures that resources are directed to priority activities in alignment with the mission plan and annual workplans. The Mission will continue to apply risk-based prioritization and adaptive planning and to strengthen management oversight to mitigate any potential risks. The impact of these measures will be closely monitored and adjusted as necessary to sustain effective mandate implementation.
D.	Regional mission cooperation

41.	During the 2024/25 period, MINUSCA strengthened regional cooperation by leveraging support services from various United Nations entities. The Mission engaged the services of the Entebbe Support Base of the United Nations Organization Stabilization Mission in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (MONUSCO), the Global Procurement Support Section and the Regional Service Centre in Entebbe, Uganda, to enhance operational efficiency. These entities provided critical support in areas that included onboarding and separation, payroll and benefits, vendor payments, entitlements, official travel, claims processing, cashier services, training and movement control.

42.	MINUSCA utilized the United Nations Logistics Base at Brindisi, Italy, for stock deployment services, streamlining procurement and ensuring timely delivery of mission supplies. In addition, MONUSCO provided two air assets on a cost-recovery basis to support MINUSCA flights, thereby contributing to improved staff well-being.

43.	The Mission continued to collaborate with the United Nations Regional Office for Central Africa, particularly on political engagement and regional initiatives addressing the situation in the Central African Republic. As part of this partnership, MINUSCA supported the preparation of the 58th meeting of the United Nations Standing Advisory Committee on Security Questions in Central Africa, held in Bangui in November 2024, by facilitating logistical arrangements and processing participant payments.

44.	During the 2026/27 period, MINUSCA will facilitate and support mediation and coordination initiatives in priority border areas, particularly with the Sudan, through engagement with local representatives and community leaders to foster trust and mitigate risks in the absence of formal government counterparts. The Mission will work closely with the United Nations country team and United Nations agencies in neighbouring countries to ensure a coherent, strategic approach to the Sudan context through strengthened information-sharing, joint situational analyses and coordinated planning.

45.	MINUSCA will maintain close collaboration with regional and subregional organizations to advance political dialogue, preventive diplomacy and early warning initiatives. Partnerships with regional and subregional organizations, including the African Union and the Economic Community of Central African States, will continue to reinforce joint efforts to promote peace, security and good governance in the Central African Republic and the wider subregion. These partnerships are essential to ensuring that regional dynamics are integrated into the Mission’s strategic planning and that political, security and programmatic interventions remain coherent and mutually reinforcing.

F.	Proposed staffing changes
Abolishment

46.	In line with ongoing efficiency efforts, which will continue to be implemented in the 2026/27 period, the proposed abolishments detailed in the table below reflect the Mission’s approach to organizational optimization and functional streamlining. Following a review of operational requirements and workload distribution, functions will be consolidated and responsibilities redistributed; these reconfigurations will support a more integrated and agile operating model aligned with evolving Mission priorities. Accordingly, it is proposed that 81 posts and positions, as detailed in the table below, be abolished.

47.	In accordance with the mandate framework governing MINUSCA electoral support, the Mission’s electoral assistance was time bound and limited to specific phases of the electoral process. As the Mission’s operational focus shifts following the successful completion of the electoral cycle and the conclusion of its electoral support mandate by 30 June 2026, the Mission’s priorities have shifted towards stabilization, the protection of civilians and governance. The functions formerly performed by the Electoral Affairs Service are no longer required beyond the current electoral support phase. No residual technical, advisory or operational responsibilities related to electoral support are foreseen within the Mission’s mandate. Accordingly, it is proposed that 67 temporary positions within the Service, and 3 posts in the Political Affairs Division, as detailed in the table below, be abolished.
Redeployment
Office of the Deputy Special Representative of the Secretary-General (Resident Coordinator/Humanitarian Coordinator)

48.	It is proposed that one position of Victims’ Rights Officer (United Nations Volunteer) be redeployed from the Office of the Special Representative of the Secretary-General to strengthen the Mission’s capacity to support victims’ rights in a more operationally focused manner. As the Mission adapts its structure to evolving mandate priorities, the proposed redeployment will ensure more effective coordination within the Mission, enhance service delivery to victims and optimize the use of existing human resources without additional financial implications.
Office of the Deputy Special Representative of the Secretary-General (Protection and Operations)

49.	It is proposed that one post of Gender Affairs Officer (P-3), three posts of Gender Affairs Officer (National Professional Officer), one post of Associate Gender Affairs Officer (National Professional Officer) and one post of Administrative Assistant (national General Service) be redeployed from the Office of the Special Representative of the Secretary-General. These posts support the integration of gender equality and women’s empowerment across all aspects of mandate implementation by advising senior leadership, providing technical guidance and ensuring that Mission policies and operations reflect the distinct needs of women, men, girls and boys.

50.	The proposed redeployment of these positions is intended to strengthen coherence across protection functions and improve alignment among components responsible for the protection of civilians, human rights, child protection, conflict-related sexual violence and gender mainstreaming. It will facilitate joint analysis and programming, as well as the systematic integration of gender perspectives into protection priorities. It will also streamline reporting and advisory channels, reinforce delivery of the Mission’s women and peace and security commitments, and enhance the effectiveness, efficiency and overall impact of the Mission’s protection architecture.
Office of the Chief of Staff

51.	It is proposed that one post of Political Affairs Officer (P-4) be redeployed from the Office of the Special Representative of the Secretary-General. The Office of the Chief of Staff provides central coordination across all Mission components, ensuring coherence, effective management and unified mandate implementation. It oversees a front office, chairs key coordination mechanisms and supports integrated planning and information flow among civilian, military and police elements. The proposed redeployment is intended to strengthen the Mission’s capacity for cross-cutting coordination, mission planning, strategic reporting, decision-tracking and liaison with senior leadership and field elements, thereby enhancing overall coherence and follow-up on strategic priorities.

52.	It is proposed that one post of Administrative Officer (Field Service) be redeployed from the Conduct and Discipline Service to strengthen the administrative and operational support capacity of the Office of the Chief of Staff, by providing direct support to the Chief of Staff and Field Office Coordination, as well as cross-functional support to all sections under the Office of the Chief of Staff. The post will ensure a permanent administrative presence responsible for key functions, including recruitment and human resources advisory support, budget and financial oversight, interdepartmental coordination and supervision of a support unit, providing additional support to the Special Representative of the Secretary-General’s front office as required. It will also enhance the efficiency and responsiveness of the Office of the Chief of Staff in delivering its mandate.

53.	It is proposed that one post of Light Vehicle Driver (national General Service) be redeployed from the Strategic Communications and Public Information Service to strengthen the logistical and operational efficiency of the Office of the Chief of Staff by providing dedicated driver support to the Chief of Staff and his/her front office. The post will ensure reliable and secure transportation for mission-wide activities, high-level engagements and field visits, while supporting administrative movements, document delivery and other logistical tasks. This redeployment will enhance scheduling flexibility, facilitate coordination with field offices and external stakeholders and improve confidentiality and security for sensitive movements.
Joint Coordination and Analysis Centre

54.	In response to the complex and dynamic operational environment in the Central African Republic, MINUSCA must adopt a more integrated, agile and data-driven approach to peacekeeping. The current separation between the Joint Operations Centre and the Joint Mission Analysis Centre has resulted in fragmented workflows and duplication of effort. Although both units draw on similar data sources, field inputs and digital platforms, they operate in parallel structures that limit mission-wide situational awareness and operational coherence.

55.	It is therefore proposed that the Joint Operations Centre and the Joint Mission Analysis Centre be consolidated into a single organizational unit, to be renamed the Joint Coordination and Analysis Centre. The merged unit will streamline operational and analytical functions, strengthen coordination across Mission components and improve the timeliness and quality of decision-making. The Joint Coordination and Analysis Centre will leverage digital transformation, including artificial intelligence-enabled analytics, automated incident monitoring and real-time dashboards, to provide timely, actionable insights to both Mission headquarters and field offices. This restructuring is aligned with the MINUSCA decentralization strategy by equipping field teams with enhanced tools, autonomy and analytical capacity to respond more effectively to evolving local dynamics.

56.	The Joint Coordination and Analysis Centre will function as the central hub for multi-source information management, integrating operational reporting with strategic assessments to support proactive planning and rapid crisis response. The consolidation will also reinforce collaboration with the Military U2 cell, the Police Operations Centre and the Security Operations Centre, ensuring that military intelligence, public safety functions and security operations are fully aligned within a unified framework. By optimizing resources, harmonizing reporting and fostering cross-functional collaboration, the Joint Coordination and Analysis Centre will enhance mission effectiveness, reduce operational redundancies and build a more resilient and adaptive mission-wide information architecture. This transformation reflects best practices in contemporary peacekeeping and positions MINUSCA to implement its mandate with greater precision, agility and impact.

57.	Accordingly, it is proposed that the posts and positions of the Joint Operations Centre and the Joint Mission Analysis Centre, detailed in the table below, be redeployed to the Joint Coordination and Analysis Centre.
Reassignment
Mission Planning Unit

58.	It is proposed that one post of Telecommunications Assistant (national General Service) in the Field Technology Section be reassigned as an Information Management Assistant to strengthen the Unit’s capacity to support data collection, analysis and visualization for strategic decision-making. The post will enhance the systematic tracking and presentation of Mission activities and financial performance, including through interactive dashboards such as the programmatic funds and strategic communications and public information dashboards, enabling real-time monitoring of project implementation and expenditure trends. By improving the consistency and accessibility of information, the role would reinforce accountability and support leadership decision-making through accurate and comprehensive data, while integrating planning, budgeting and performance monitoring within a unified, results-oriented framework.
Establishment
Office of the Deputy Special Representative of the Secretary-General (Resident Coordinator/Humanitarian Coordinator)

59.	In line with the request from the General Assembly in its resolution 76/274 for further nationalization and taking into account the proposed abolishment of one post of Senior Victims’ Rights Officer (P-5) in the Office of the Special Representative of the Secretary-General, the capacity of national staff and the potential for increased efficiencies, it is proposed that one post of Victims’ Rights Officer (National Professional Officer) be established. The proposed national post will ensure the continued delivery of victims’ rights functions in a more cost-effective and operationally focused manner. Leveraging national expertise, language proficiency and knowledge of the local context is expected to enhance direct engagement with affected communities and facilitate the timely implementation of activities, while contributing to overall budgetary efficiencies and the Mission’s nationalization objectives.
Continuation of general temporary assistance positions
Office of the Deputy Special Representative of the Secretary-General (Protection and Operations)

60.	One position of Programme Management Officer (P-4), funded under general temporary assistance, was established on 1 July 2022 and is embedded in the staffing of the Office of the Deputy Special Representative of the Secretary-General (Protection and Operations) to direct implementation of the mine action programme in MINUSCA. In view of the continuation of the programme in the 2026/27 period, it is proposed that this position be retained. The incumbent develops the Mission’s mine action programme strategy; leads operational planning, programme design and responses to threats; supports advocacy efforts; and oversees the delivery and performance of implementing partners.
Aviation Section

61.	It is proposed that 10 positions of Air Operations Assistant (national General Service), funded under general temporary assistance, be retained to operate the specialized firefighting vehicles during aircraft refuelling and engine start-up, take-off and landing.
Change of organizational unit title
Office of the Deputy Special Representative of the Secretary-General (Protection and Operations)

62.	It is proposed that the name of the Office be changed from the Office of the Deputy Special Representative of the Secretary-General (Political) to the Office of the Deputy Special Representative of the Secretary-General (Protection and Operations). The proposed change reflects an evolution in the focus of the Office, aligning it more closely with the Mission’s current priorities and operational realities. It also underscores the centrality of protection-related responsibilities within the Mission’s mandate, while clarifying leadership and accountability for protection outcomes.
Mission Planning Unit

63.	It is proposed that the name of the Unit be changed from Strategic Planning Unit to Mission Planning Unit. The proposed change better reflects contemporary United Nations policy and planning practices, as well as the Mission’s evolving operational realities. As the Mission continues to adapt its structures to shifting mandate priorities, this change will align the function more closely with integrated, forward-looking and results-oriented planning processes. It will also enhance clarity of purpose across Mission components, strengthen coordination and ensure that planning frameworks remain responsive, practical and operationally relevant, without entailing additional resource implications.

64.	The following evaluations were completed by the Office of Internal Oversight Services (OIOS) during the 2024/25 period:
(a)	Evaluation of protection of civilians, security and human rights in the United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in the Central African Republic (IED-25-018);
(b)	Triennial review report on the implementation of OIOS recommendations in the report on the evaluation of political affairs in peacekeeping missions (IED-25-013);
(c)	Evaluation of children and armed conflict in peacekeeping operations (IED-25-005).

65.	The Mission has duly noted the findings of the above-referenced evaluations. While these evaluations provide valuable thematic insights, the Mission considers that a focused assessment of the implementation of the mission plan would yield greater strategic value, given the robust results framework guiding Mission priorities. Recognizing that ongoing liquidity constraints have at times limited full implementation of recommendations from thematic evaluations, the Mission underscores the importance of assessments that take into account both operational realities and the financial context. Such evaluations would enhance evidence-based decision-making, inform resource allocation and support adaptive strategies to maximize the effectiveness and impact of Mission activities.

66.	In addition, MINUSCA relies on semi-annual assessments conducted by the Comprehensive Planning and Performance Assessment System team against a set of indicators drawn from the mission plan’s outputs and outcomes. These indicators, now integrated at the outcome level into the present report, enable progress measurement and trend identification and provide leadership with data-driven analysis for informed decision-making. The integration of these indicators will also facilitate the use of infographics to present findings more clearly and accessibly. These assessments are well positioned to feed lessons learned into the Mission’s planning process.

71.	Credits comprising the unencumbered balances, other revenue and the cancellation of prior-period obligations reduce the assessed contributions owed by Member States in the following financial year. The credit for the 2024/25 period for overall peacekeeping operations amounts to $335.5 million, including the support entities and the United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in Mali. In the current financial situation, offsetting this large amount of credit against a future assessment for the 2026/27 period will reduce the collection and undermine the ability of peacekeeping operations to fully implement their mandates. Accordingly, the Secretary-General proposes that the General Assembly approve that the credits in respect of the financial period 2024/25 be placed in a reserve that would be used in the event that collections are insufficient to enable the implementation of those mandates; and allow Member States to apply their share towards current or future dues once the Assembly determines that the liquidity situation has sufficiently improved. This proposal is explained in more detail in the peacekeeping overview report.

74.	The vacancy factors take into account the high number of abolishments included in the proposed budget for 2026/27. Accordingly, the proposed vacancy rates for civilian personnel categories with abolishments were estimated using the total number of vacant posts and positions as at 31 December 2025, net of the number of posts and positions proposed for abolishment. For categories without proposed abolishments, the standard policy guidance was applied, using either the 12-month average vacancy rate from January to December 2025 or the actual vacancy rate as at 31 December 2025. For military and police personnel, the vacancy factors reflect their planned deployment during the 2026/27 period. This approach ensures that vacancy rates are based on, to the extent possible, the actual data while maintaining consistency across the proposed budgets. A vacancy rate of 50 per cent has been applied in calculating the costs of the proposed establishment and reassignment of posts.

As described in the planning assumptions for the 2026/27 period, after the successful conduct of the combined presidential, legislative, regional and municipal elections on 28 December 2025, MINUSCA began to adjust its footprint and posture in line with its mandate under Security Council resolution 2800 (2025). In the post-election context, MINUSCA proposes to repurpose the former 1,678 electoral flight hours to strengthen support for the Armed Forces of the Central African Republic and the internal security forces, given that the Mission is initiating a phase of consolidation, reconfiguration and transition.

81.	The proposed resource requirements for the 2026/27 period will amount to $4,000,000.

82.	Through quick-impact projects, the Mission will continue to create an enabling environment that strengthens peace, security and State authority across the Central African Republic. Quick-impact projects will enhance the Mission’s presence, build trust with local communities and support political, peace and reconciliation processes by delivering basic social services, promoting socioeconomic empowerment initiatives for women, young people and vulnerable populations and facilitating the reintegration of ex-combatants. The Mission will also support the sustainable restoration of State authority by rehabilitating public administrative infrastructure, providing equipment, strengthening local governance, promoting human rights, enhancing the justice system and supporting security deployments. Furthermore, quick-impact projects will improve security conditions and civilian protection by increasing the mobility and presence of national and MINUSCA security forces, reinforcing early warning and communication systems, restoring freedom of movement, mitigating local conflicts and facilitating humanitarian access. All projects will be implemented in coordination with national authorities, United Nations agencies, humanitarian organizations and local communities, ensuring grass-roots participation, local ownership and sustainable impact across the seven proposed regions.
