117.	The reduced requirements were offset in part by higher costs for services, resulting from demobilization and de-positioning costs associated with the termination of the commercial contract for the unmanned aerial systems, for which no provision had been included in the approved budget.

118.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) the anticipated lower costs for two fixed-wing aircraft (DHC-8-400 and DHC-8-311), resulting from changes in contractual arrangements, which affect both fixed costs and cost per flight hour, compared with the estimates in the approved budget; (b) the anticipated lower costs for one MI-17 helicopter, resulting from changes in contractual arrangements, which affect the cost per flight hour, compared with the estimates in the approved budget; (c) the lower anticipated consumption of 9.7 million litres of aviation fuel at an average cost of $1.330 per litre compared with 10.9 million litres in the approved budget, as a result of the scheduled completion of the Mission’s electoral mandate by 30 June 2026; and (d) the anticipated lower expenditure for landing fees and ground handling charges, based on historical expenditure patterns.

119.	For the 2024/25 period, the increased requirements were attributable primarily to the acquisition of additional sea containers to ensure the timely and secure transportation of infrastructure materials and prefabricated structures to designated field locations.

120.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) lower-than-anticipated costs for public information and publication services, due to the scaling down of production and outreach activities as a result of liquidity constraints; (b) the non-acquisition of communications and information technology equipment, due to the transfer of equipment from MINUSMA and other available resources; (c) lower actual costs for spare parts, due to effective preventive maintenance, the re-use of existing components and the non-acquisition of satellite imagery; and (d) lower actual costs for telecommunications and network services, resulting from the partial implementation of bandwidth upgrades, lower subscription cost and transponder rates, and reduced satellite radio streaming due to liquidity constraints.

121.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to lower anticipated expenditures across various areas, including: (a) promotional and outreach activities, following the scheduled completion of the Mission’s electoral mandate by 30 June 2026; (b) the lower rates for centrally managed information and communications technology services and the cancellation of the long-term evolution (LTE) expansion to Bambari and Ndélé; and (c) maintenance and technical support, reflecting the planned termination of specialized server room contracts and the transition of videoconferencing and Terrestrial Trunked Radio (TETRA) services to the United Nations Logistics Base at a reduced cost.

122.	The reduced requirements are offset in part by increased requirements, related primarily to the strengthening of the Mission’s information technology and communications infrastructure, including enhancements to mission-wide power protection, connectivity and communications systems, as well as the planned deployment of new technologies to improve operational resilience and support critical mission activities.

123.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to the availability of sufficient inventory stocks, which resulted in the acquisition of fewer vaccines, pharmaceuticals and patient care products; the non-acquisition of first-aid kits, in line with a Headquarters directive restricting pharmaceutical procurement to established contracts; and the deferral of acquisition of selected medical and hygiene supplies owing to liquidity constraints.

124.	For the 2024/25 period, the increased requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) higher actual costs for freight and related services, due to the unplanned transportation of materials, equipment and prefabricated structures to improve warehouse conditions in field locations, as well as additional in-land cargo transport to field locations; and (b) administrative and management fees payable under the agreement with the United Nations Office for Project Services for the management of individual contractual personnel, for which no provision was included in the approved budget.

125.	The increased requirements were offset in part by lower costs for: (a) mine detection and mine clearing services, due to the cancellation of various planned pre-deployment training sessions, weapons and ammunition management infrastructure activities, and training sessions, due to liquidity constraints; (b) training fees, supplies and services, owing to lower-than-anticipated trainer travel and limited on-site training sessions due to liquidity constraints; and (c) bank charges, owing to delays in establishing two branches.

126.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to higher costs associated with the implementation of paragraph 48 of Security Council resolution 2800 (2025), including: (a) freight costs related to the transport of additional quantities of equipment and supplies from field locations scheduled for closure as part of the consolidation and reconfiguration of MINUSCA in the post-election context (internal movement of goods); and (b) the provision of combat ration packs for the Armed Forces of the Central African Republic for consumption during joint operations.

127.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to the lower actual number of projects implemented compared with the number of projects in the approved in the budget, due to liquidity constraints.

text: IV.	Actions to be taken by the General Assembly

children.text: 128.	The actions to be taken by the General Assembly in connection with the financing of the United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in the Central African Republic are:
(a)	To take note of the unencumbered balance of $25,350,600 with respect to the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025;

(b)	To take note of other revenue for the period ended 30 June 2025 amounting to $21,749,200 from investment revenue ($11,502,200), other/miscellaneous revenue ($811,500) and the cancellation of prior-period obligations ($9,435,500);

(c)	To approve the apportionment of the amount of $47,099,800 among Member States in accordance with the applicable scale of assessments for 2025 and to place such apportioned amount in a reserve that would be used in the event that collections are insufficient to enable the full, efficient and effective implementation of mandates;

(d)	To allow Member States to apply their share of the amount apportioned in subparagraph (c) above towards current or future dues once the General Assembly determines that the liquidity situation has improved sufficiently to allow such application;
(e)	To appropriate the amount of $1,021,635,400 for the maintenance of the Mission for the 12-month period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027;
(f)	To assess the amount in subparagraph (e) above at a monthly rate of $85,136,284 should the Security Council decide to continue the mandate of the Mission.

text: V.	Summary of follow-up action taken to implement the decisions and requests of the General Assembly in its resolutions 76/274 and 79/302, including the requests and recommendations of the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions endorsed by the Assembly

children.text: A.	General Assembly
Cross-cutting issues
(Resolution 76/274)

table_content.text: Requests the Secretary-General to improve comprehensive oversight of the activities of peacekeeping missions and implement the recommendations of relevant oversight bodies in this regard to avoid deficiencies in management and related economic losses, with the aim of ensuring full compliance with the Financial Regulations and Rules of the United Nations (para. 17).

As at 31 December 2025, 224 of the 299 recommendations (75 per cent) issued by the Office of Internal Oversight Services and 42 of the 65 recommendations (65 per cent) issued by the Board of Auditors were implemented. Regular follow-ups are undertaken to ensure the timely closure of the remaining recommendations.

Requests that future budget proposals demonstrate the scalability of mission support components, including their staffing and operational costs, be proportionate in relation to the changing level of other mission components and include standard indicators (para. 27).

The mission support ratio (the total number of mission support and security personnel per 1,000 total mission personnel) in the 2025/26 period is 51.5, lower than the figure of 59.6 for the 2020/21 period. The Mission will continue to take mission support scalability into consideration in future budget reports.

Requests the Secretary-General to consider options for greater nationalization of functions when formulating budget submissions, commensurate with mission mandates and requirements where applicable (para. 34).

In response to the General Assembly’s request to consider options for greater nationalization of functions, MINUSCA continues to advance a structured approach to the nationalization of suitable functions, commensurate with mandate requirements and operational needs.
In this regard, the Mission is implementing a 2024–2028 nationalization strategy that provides for the progressive transition of selected international functions to national positions, including through the conversion of vacant posts, natural attrition and targeted capacity-building initiatives. These measures are designed to maintain operational continuity, enhance cost efficiency and support the sustainable development of national capacities, while informing the formulation of budget submissions, as applicable. For the 2026/27 period, it is proposed that a victims’ rights function be nationalized in the Office of the Deputy Special Representative of the Secretary-General (Resident Coordinator/ Humanitarian Coordinator).

Recalls the provisions of section XVIII of its resolution 61/276, recognizes the important role played by quick-impact projects in establishing and building confidence in missions, further recognizes the importance of conducting assessments of the quick-impact projects regularly, on their needs and impact as required in resolution 61/276, requests the Secretary-General to include the conducted assessments in his next reports, and also requests the Secretary-General to enhance their impact (para. 82).

In line with General Assembly resolution 61/276, quick-impact projects continue to play a key role in building confidence between local communities, national authorities and the Mission, and in supporting stabilization and peacebuilding efforts in the Central African Republic.
The Mission conducts regular assessments of quick-impact projects to evaluate their relevance, outcomes and impact, ensuring continued alignment with mandate priorities and community needs. The findings of these assessments have been reflected in reports of the Secretary-General, in accordance with the Assembly’s request, and demonstrate the tangible contribution of quick-impact projects to local stabilization and early peace dividends.
On the basis of assessment results, MINUSCA has taken steps to enhance the impact of quick-impact projects by prioritizing projects that deliver visible and immediate benefits, including the rehabilitation and construction of essential infrastructure such as roads, administrative buildings, schools and health facilities. These interventions have improved access to basic services, strengthened community resilience and supported the re-establishment of State authority, thereby reinforcing confidence in the Mission and its mandate.

children.text: B.	Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions
Financing of the United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in the Central African Republic

table_content.text: The Advisory Committee trusts that information on expenditure related to electoral support during the 2024/25 period, taking into account the aforementioned revised timelines, will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and in the next budget proposal (para. 17).
Election-related expenditure during the 2024/25 period amounted to $12.0 million.

The Advisory Committee notes the proposed restructuring of the MINUSCA mission support structure and trusts that there will be a period of stability in that regard. The Committee trusts that updated information on any efficiencies and lessons learned from the exercise will be reflected in the next programme budget submission (para. 29).

The recent restructuring of the Mission Support Division of MINUSCA has improved efficiency and operational effectiveness and is intended to provide a stable support structure going forward. The Mission is monitoring the new configuration to consolidate gains, ensure continuity and avoid further structural changes except where operationally necessary.
Initial efficiencies and lessons learned are already emerging. For example, the integration of occupational health and safety under the services delivery pillar has facilitated more effective resolution of safety-related issues through improved alignment with the Medical Services Section. Similarly, the repositioning of the Claims Unit within the supply chain management pillar has streamlined access to critical data, as that pillar oversees acquisitions, property management, vendors and claims. Placing the Environmental Unit directly under the Director of Mission Support has elevated its strategic role, strengthening environmental compliance across the Mission. These revised reporting lines have supported improved planning and project implementation.
In addition, the relocation of the Conference and Translation Unit to the Integrated Mission Training Cell has enhanced productivity, visibility, innovation and day-to-day operations, while also promoting multilingualism through the development of lexical resources to support learning and language proficiency.
The Mission will continue to assess the impact of the restructuring, and updated information on efficiencies achieved and lessons learned will be reflected in the next programme budget submission.

The Advisory Committee trusts that information on the status of vacant posts, as well as on the impact of the liquidity situation on projected hirings, will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and in the next budget submission (para. 30).

The trend in vacancy rates in MINUSCA between July and December 2025 is as follows:



During this period, the Mission experienced a steady increase in vacancy rates, in particular among international staff. The prevailing liquidity situation has constrained the Mission’s ability to proceed with projected recruitments, resulting in delays in filling critical posts and associated impacts on operational delivery. The Mission continues to closely monitor staffing levels and recruitment pipelines, and updated information on vacancy rates and the impact of liquidity constraints on projected hirings will be reflected in the next budget submission.

The Advisory Committee recalls paragraph 34 of General Assembly resolution 76/274 (see also A/78/744, para. 37) and is of the view that more efforts should be made to nationalize functions commensurate with mission mandates and requirements, wherever appropriate. The Committee underscores the importance of building national capacity and recommends that the Assembly request MINUSCA to enhance its efforts to implement the civilian staffing review recommendation on further nationalizations and to include proposals in that regard in the next budget proposals (para. 33).

In response to the General Assembly’s request to explore options for greater nationalization of functions, MINUSCA continues to implement a structured approach to the nationalization of suitable functions, in line with mandate requirements and operational needs.
The Mission is implementing a 2024–2028 nationalization strategy that provides for the progressive transition of selected international functions to national positions, through the conversion of vacant posts, natural attrition and targeted capacity-building initiatives. These measures are intended to maintain operational continuity, enhance cost efficiency and strengthen the sustainable development of national capacities, while informing the formulation of future budget submissions. For the 2026/27 period, it is proposed that a victims’ rights function be nationalized in the Office of the Deputy Special Representative of the Secretary-General (Resident Coordinator/Humanitarian Coordinator).
Due to the ongoing liquidity constraints, which have led to a temporary freeze on recruitment, the implementation of certain nationalization measures has been postponed. Nevertheless, it is proposed that 74 international positions be abolished, thereby enhancing cost efficiency. MINUSCA intends to resume implementation of the nationalization strategy in the 2027/28 fiscal year, at which time measurable targets and milestones will be established and reflected in future budget proposals.

The Advisory Committee trusts that further information, including a cost comparison and related lessons learned, of utilizing hard-walled buildings instead of prefabricated structures will be included in the next budget proposal. The Committee emphasizes the importance of ensuring the integration of locally sourced and manufactured materials, as well as local labour and expertise, when possible, and trusts that updates thereon will be provided in future budget proposals (para. 40).

The Mission conducted an initial cost comparison between prefabricated buildings and hard-walled structures. The analysis indicates that the annual cost of operating a six-module prefabricated building (1,008 m²) is approximately $11,377, whereas a hard-walled structure of comparable size costs about $5,600 per year, representing a cost reduction of nearly 51 per cent.
From an operational perspective, prefabricated structures generate additional costs owing to international import requirements, extended timelines for fabrication and delivery to the Central African Republic, and accelerated deterioration resulting from harsh climatic conditions. By contrast, hard-walled buildings benefit from the use of locally sourced materials and labour. Their relatively straightforward construction methods and ease of skills transfer have supported rapid capacity development, created local employment opportunities and increased demand for domestic materials, thereby contributing positively to the construction sector in the Central African Republic.
The Advisory Committee trusts that information on the guidelines, as well as on the cost-recovery formula, will be provided in the next budget proposal (para. 41).

Guidelines to enhance the optimal utilization of the multi-purpose building are currently being developed by the Mission. The objective of these guidelines is to ensure the facility functions effectively as a multifunctional platform supporting meetings, training activities, guest accommodation and engagement with partners. Upon finalization, the guidelines will establish clear procedures and operational standards to promote efficient, transparent and equitable utilization of the facility by all stakeholders.
A draft cost-recovery formula for the utilization of the multi-purpose building has been developed in close coordination with key internal stakeholders and will be in effect in the 2026/2027 period. Upon approval, the proposed formula will be submitted for configuration in the cost-recovery system of the Organization with a clear activity matrix. In the meantime, the Mission is systematically documenting all conferences and events conducted in the facility to ensure accurate and effective application of the cost-recovery framework once approval is granted. The implementation of this mechanism will strengthen financial accountability and contribute to the sustainable operation of the facility.

The Advisory Committee considers that the full implementation of the new contracts for the outsourcing of maintenance of facilities and camp services may not be ready with effect as of the beginning of the 2025/26 period. The Committee trusts that updated information on the status of procurement of the new contracts for facilities maintenance and camp management services will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and presented in the next budget proposal, including on the actual start date of the outsourcing contracts and on any related financial impact on the proposed resources (para. 44).

The solicitation process for the outsourcing of facilities maintenance and camp management services was completed and approved by the Headquarters Committee on Contracts in May 2025. The award of the contracts was subsequently delayed due to a combination of liquidity constraints and risk-management considerations, particularly those related to the transition of individual contractors during the electoral period in the Central African Republic. Given the elevated political and operational risk at the time, MINUSCA determined that proceeding with contract awards and transitions would have entailed undue operational and financial exposure.
Following the successful conduct of national elections on 28 December 2025 and the corresponding reduction in political risk, the Mission focused on a phased transition plan, with full activation of the outsourcing contracts expected by December 2026. Interim arrangements remain in place to ensure continuity of facilities maintenance and camp services pending completion of the transition.
Updated information on the status of these contracts, including the actual start date and any related financial impacts, will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and included in the next budget submission, in line with the Advisory Committee’s request.

The Advisory Committee trusts that MINUSCA will ensure that projects benefit the local population as intended and that updated information, including on planning, implementation and assessment, will be provided in the context of the next budget proposal (para. 51).

MINUSCA ensures that projects are designed and implemented in line with the needs of the local population through its established planning and results framework. This framework provides an overview of priority needs and tracks progress against indicators linked to the outputs of the mission plan, allowing for the ongoing assessment of relevance, effectiveness and impact.
Planning, implementation and monitoring information will continue to be updated and reflected in future proposed budget reports, in line with the Advisory Committee’s request. While the Mission does not currently apply a standardized accountability to affected populations strategy, the needs of the local population are systematically considered through existing planning, coordination and monitoring mechanisms.

The Advisory Committee notes the efforts of the Mission to improve asset and inventory management and looks forward to further information on the integration of the supply chain tool as a baseline for budget preparation to be presented in the next budget report (para. 55).

The Mission uses the outputs of its supply chain planning tools, driven by a rolling demand process, as the analytical baseline for resource planning and material requirements planning. This approach aligns operational requirements with available resources and allows for adjustments in response to liquidity constraints. The outputs are updated quarterly using validated data on inventory levels, consumption trends, asset condition and service demand. They inform demand prioritization, replenishment decisions and the timing of acquisitions and disposals.

The Advisory Committee notes the efforts of the Mission to improve asset and inventory management and looks forward to further information on the integration of the supply chain tool as a baseline for budget preparation to be presented in the next budget report (para. 55).
Updated information on the integration and use of the supply chain planning tools will continue to be reflected in future budget reports, in line with the Advisory Committee’s request.
